speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 23, 1995. The survivor is Mr. Henry Wellner. This is tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So in 1962, I got sick. Oh, wait a minute. Wait a minute. I lived with my brother all the time. My brother married, had a child, and in 1957 he died. He had a heart attack. I saw him in the morning when I was leaving for my work. I was in the street. [INAUDIBLE]. I loved it. I had my route. I had about 250, 300 people. I made a nice living. And in 1957, I saw him in the morning. Abraham, I saw him in the morning. He went to his business. And around 9 o'clock in the evening where the spot-- in 1957, quite a few boys were-- single boys-- were at the spot in a gas station. We played cards over there, went to eat together. Across the street was a Jewish kosher restaurant. A guy comes over and he tells me, you know, Abraham died.

speaker: interviewer
text: My reaction was, what is the guy talking? I didn't have no reaction, because I saw him in the morning. But later on-- 1957, my brother died. He left a daughter. She was at that time five years old. My sister-in-law went to New York. She remarried over there. And I continued in Chicago until 1973 or 1974.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Miami for vacation, and it happens that I have a good friend from my old town. Also a good friend of [? Bluma's. ?] Her husband. Her former husband. And they made a date and said, yeah, and that was it. We got married a year later. The boys-- I mean, my friends-- somehow they don't want to talk to me no more, because they became very wealthy people. But in Chicago I had a lot of friends, mostly married. They made of party for us, you know, in a place in a nightclub. It must have come about 100. Each one was very happy to see me to be married. And from there we went to Boston, because [? Bluma ?] had two children over there. The other one came to Boston. And Rabbi [? Hobbs, ?] he was [INAUDIBLE]. And when I came to live here, the change was just horrible. First of all, the climate. I hate today the hot climate. At that time, the change was so drastically different. I didn't have no job. And eventually, I got a job. And Bluma, she's a good chef. She got a job here. After a certain time, she got a job here. The last of her job was by [? Conover. ?] [? Conover ?] Hotel. This [? Conover ?] was a wonderful, wonderful man. He was like a father to her. And somebody else took over the-- he died. [? Conover ?] died. Somebody else took over. Bluma-- in the meantime, Bluma got sick. Cancer. It was hell going to Washington for two years. And here I was with her all the time. It was no picnic. It was no picnic. It was very bad. And God bless, she's fine. She's in remission the sixth year, and I'm here. 1975-- wait, wait. 1985, I had open heart surgery. And [INAUDIBLE] 10 years. I don't want to even say how long it is. I had my ups and downs. For the last three years or four years, I have a lot of trouble with my back. A lot of trouble. I can't walk too much. I have to walk. And I can't sit too long. I can't stay too long. [INAUDIBLE]. But somehow life is going on, and got to thank for the far. I see my brother once in awhile. We go to Israel. Last time I couldn't go to Israel. Bluma went to Israel, because I couldn't. I figured out that eight, nine hours-- I couldn't be in the plane for eight, nine hours. She went for 10 days and that's the truth. That's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank, Mr. Wellner, for participating in the survivors-- You're welcome. You're welcome. Thank you very much. Thank you. What a wonderful-- OK. Mr. Wellner, please introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Bluma Wellner. And I love her. And we're going to be almost 20 years together next year. And I wish to have another beautiful 20 years together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's good?

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thank you. Mr. Wellner, please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. This was my sister, [? Topsha. ?] She was born in Poland. She lived all her life in Dabrowa Gornicza, and she perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wellner, please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my beautiful, wonderful sister, [? Topsha ?] Wellner. She was born in 1915, and she lived her whole life in Dombrowa. She was just a very modern woman. You can see from the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken in 1938 or 1939. Something like that. And she perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wellner, please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my oldest brother, [? Moshi, ?] with his wife. He was born in 1912. The picture was taken in the town of [? Wyzsza ?] where he lived. He also perished in Auschwitz with his wife. This was my youngest brother with the name [? Jakob. ?] He was born in 1927 in Dabrowa Gornicza. And he perished. He died in Auschwitz. That's my brother Abraham, born 1918. I lived with him in Chicago. This is his wife. He married her in New York. He died in Chicago and she lives in New York. That's it. That's my niece, Annette. The picture is taken in Brandeis University. She lives in New York. This is my brother Shmuel who was born in 1916, and he lives in Chicago. And those are his two grandchildren. This is my nephew [? Emil. ?] This picture was taken the day he came out from the Army. This was I think in 1960-something. He lives in Chicago, and he has two wonderful children, a daughter of 23 years old-- she goes to college-- and a son of 19-- something like that. 19 or 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose son is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emil's. Emil has two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is his father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Schmuel's. Shmuel's. Yeah. He's the son of Shmuel, Shmuel [? Remna. ?] This picture is taken from a wedding for my nephew [? Liam. ?] He is the son of Shmuel. And in the picture there is my nephew [? Liam, ?] his wife Rivka, my wife Bluma, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- how many years ago? I'd say-- wait a minute. Oh, this was taken about 15 years ago, I think. She's already-- 15 years ago. In Chicago. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.
